note_data.title: The Periodic Table
keyPoints: Filtration separates insoluble solid from liquid because liquid particles are small enough to go through filter paper pores while solid particles are too large
Crystallisation is used for obtaining solid from solid & liquid solution or formation of crystals from a cooling liquid/saturated solution
Distillation obtains pure liquid from solution - pure liquid has low BP and changes to gas easily while solid does not boil and has high BP
Fractional Distillation separates 2 liquids which are mixed (miscible) - liquid with lowest BP comes out first at top of fractionating column
Chromatography separates mixture of organic compounds (eg. colour dyes)
In filtration, solid obtained is called 'Residue' and liquid obtained is called 'Filtrate'
In distillation and fractional distillation, liquid obtained is called 'Distillate' and is collected in conical flask
A saturated solution means no more solid can dissolve in it
Compounds contain 2 or more elements chemically joined together and contain covalent or ionic bonds
Mixtures are 2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together and can consist of elements, compounds, or elements + compounds

definitions.definition: 2 or more elements chemically joined together. Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together
No more solid can dissolve in it

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use trends to suggest properties of unknown elements
State similarities AND differences between elements or groups

modelAnswer.question: Explain why atomic radius decreases across Period 3 from sodium to chlorine.
modelAnswer.answer: Across Period 3, the nuclear charge increases from +11 to +17. The number of electron shells remains the same (3 shells). The increased nuclear charge pulls the electrons more strongly towards the nucleus, making the atomic radius smaller.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Number of electron shells stays the same
Mark 3: Stronger attraction pulls electrons closer/atomic radius decreases

commonMistakes: Confusing pure substances with mixtures - pure substances have fixed composition and fixed Mp/Bp while mixtures have variable composition and variable Bp/Mp
Not understanding that any question asking for method to obtain crystals will use crystallisation process
Forgetting that the constant temperature during distillation equals the boiling point of pure liquid
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystallisation'
Remember that soluble means can dissolve in water (eg. Salt) and insoluble means cannot dissolve in water (eg. Sand)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Periodic table showing 18 groups (vertical columns) and 7 periods (horizontal rows), with metals on the left, non-metals on the right, and metalloids along the diagonal line
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to identify the correct arrangement of elements in groups and periods
N/J 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — required knowledge of how atomic number determines position in periodic table
M/J 2021 P2 Q5a (3 marks) — students had to explain why elements in the same group have similar properties
N/J 2020 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — tested understanding of period number relation to electron shells
M/J 2019 P2 Q4c (2 marks) — asked to predict properties of unknown element based on periodic table position

workedExample.question: Element X has atomic number 17. Use the periodic table to determine: (a) the group number, (b) the period number, (c) the number of electrons in the outer shell. [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Identify that atomic number 17 means 17 protons and 17 electrons
Step 2: Write electron configuration: 2,8,7 (2 in first shell, 8 in second shell, 7 in outer shell)
Step 3: Group number = number of electrons in outer shell = 7 (Group VII)
Step 4: Period number = number of electron shells = 3 (Period 3)
workedExample.answer: (a) Group VII/Group 17 (b) Period 3 (c) 7 electrons in outer shell
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct group, 1 mark for correct period, 1 mark for correct outer electrons, 1 mark for showing working/electron arrangement

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused group number with period number' — always remember group is vertical columns, period is horizontal rows
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students incorrectly stated that elements in same period have identical properties' — elements in same GROUP have similar properties, not same period
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates failed to link electron arrangement to periodic table position' — always show electron configuration when explaining element position
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a simplified periodic table showing: Groups I-VIII labeled vertically (columns), Periods 1-3 labeled horizontally (rows), positions of first 20 elements with their symbols, and clear indication that group number = outer shell electrons, period number = number of electron shells. Include hydrogen in its unique position and show the transition between metals (left) and non-metals (right).
note_data.quickRecap: Separation methods depend on the properties of substances being separated. Filtration uses particle size differences, crystallisation obtains solids from solutions, distillation separates based on boiling point differences, and chromatography separates organic compounds. Pure substances have fixed properties while mixtures have variable properties and can be separated by physical methods.
